More generally, I'm a little concerned about the amount of imperative (as opposed to declarative) logic in this function (or perhaps in general? that's entirely unfounded though). However, pragmatically speaking, I doubt that's actually a problem in any tangible way.

Since I was the person to ask, here is more context of what I was asking. I think the fastest way to explain it is with an user case. Let's say that me, a gdbgui user, wants to add a tab to gdbgui. So, I look in the wiki and there's the following sample code I can extend to my needs. This fiction example code takes an array name, an array size and renders the content of the named array as a html table. ```python from gdbgui import Tab import gdb class ExampleTab(Tab): '''This prints an array provided its name and size Analog to `(gdb) print *some_array@size` ''' tabCounter = 0 # form fields, picked up and filled auto-magically array_name = StringField() array_size = UnsignedIntegerField() def __init__(self, *args, **kargs): self.myTabId = self.__class__.tabCounter self.__class__.tabCounter += 1 def name(self): '''This name must be unique among tabs. There may be multiple instances of this same tab, so the implementer must handle their names''' return 'example {0}'.format(self.__class__.tabCounter) def _get_array_values(self): '''Returns the n=array_size values of array_name as an int list''' pointer_to_data = gdb.parse_and_eval(self.array_name) return [int(gdb.parse_and_eval('*({array_name)+{i})'.format( array_name=str(self.array_name), i=i) for i in range(0,int(self.array_size))] def render(self): '''Render the array as a one-column html table''' return '{0}
'.format( '{0}'.join( self._get_array_values())) ``` Now, I don't know what's the status on this. Maybe some stuff has been already done, but no such API exist. For me, I would modify it to parse the points of a named `boost::polygon_data`, give the points to (let's say) plotly and return the generated html in `render()`. This would be something like the first option @cs01 described, with a little bit of the 4th.

I have never tested with a gdb version earlier than 7.6, so this might be the cause of the issue. Are you trying to view memory in gdbgui? I don't _think_ it should have any impact on call stack display since gdbgui handles memory responses completely independently from call stack responses.

Do you mean that you want to define your augmentation sequence once, apply all augmenters to one input, but only some augmenters to another? In that case you can use hooks to dynamically deactivate some augmenters during execution. Something like:
```python
seq = iaa.Sequential([
    iaa.GaussianBlur((0.1, 2.0), name="GaussianBlur"),
    iaa.Dropout(0.02, name="Dropout"),
    iaa.Affine(rotate=(-35, 35), name="Affine")
])

# change the activated augmenters for heatmaps,
# we only want to execute horizontal flip, affine transformation and one of
# the gaussian noises
def activator_heatmaps(images, augmenter, parents, default):
    if augmenter.name in ["GaussianBlur", "Dropout"]:
        return False
    else:
        # default value for all other augmenters
        return default
hooks_heatmaps = ia.HooksImages(activator=activator_heatmaps)

seq_det = seq.to_deterministic() # call this for each batch again, NOT only once at the start
images_aug = seq_det.augment_images(images)
heatmaps_aug = seq_det.augment_images(heatmaps, hooks=hooks_heatmaps)
```

Hi John. Thanks for the heads up. I would indeed like to see a reference on where this was announced. The developer documentation appears to still show that access without specifying a locale is supported for many endpoints. An example would be:

https://developer.zendesk.com/rest_api/docs/help_center/articles#show-article

Also, I think that currently every endpoint which has `locale` in the URL provided in the documentation supports taking an optional `locale` parameter.

Here is what the 'show article' endpoint looks like when it's generated:

https://github.com/fprimex/zdesk/blob/master/zdesk/zdesk_api.py#L1329

Contrast this with the documented API for help center article comments, which currently has no `locale` path parameter in the URL at all. The locale is specified in the comment data structure only.

https://developer.zendesk.com/rest_api/docs/help_center/comments

So right now I think we're OK, but I think keeping this open and checking for documentation updates as we get closer to June 27 would be a good idea.

@bigmittens I think this is a great idea, especially as Spotify's default ordering is a bit... well crap.

I've lodged a request with Spotify to allow their web API to accept sort parameters, however I can't imagine this will happen quickly. The reason it needs to happen at Spotify's end is that the lazy-loading would create an unexpected user experience.

For example, sorted A-Z of 200 items. You scroll down the first 50 records (to F, let's say) and the the lazy-loader fetches the next 50 items. Some of these include items starting A-F, which would be injected further up the list. You can imagine the confusion it would create.

I'll harass Spotify again and start thinking about some alternative ways we can achieve this.

I suggest an alternative (incompatible) mounting script;
```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash

# extract the last part after '#' in 'yas3fs#bucket' string
s3bucket=${1##*#}
localpath=$2
params=()

# parse the fstab options as long parameters to yas3fs
eval "for w in ${4//,/ }; do params+=(\"--\$w\"); done"

# remove parameters to fuse
delete=( "--_netdev" "--allow_other" "--default_permissions" )
for target in "${delete[@]}"
do
# echo finding "$target" in "${#params[@]}" values
  for i in "${!params[@]}"
  do
#   echo checking "$i" "${params[i]}"
    if [ "$target" = "${params[i]}" ]
      then
#     echo unsetting "${params[i]}"
      unset 'params[i]'
#   else
#     echo "${params[i]}" is not "$target"
    fi
  done
done

scl enable python27 -- yas3fs "s3://${s3bucket}" "${localpath}" ${params[*]}
```

Here is the main issue...some platforms (Juniper, Alcatel, Cisco to a certain extent in config mode) have prompts that very frequently change.

When you call find_prompt(), it updates the self.router_prompt attribute (and self.router_prompt is used as part of the send_command() method to strip off the trailing prompt at the end of show commands).  If self.router_prompt doesn't match the current real prompt, then this stripping the trailing prompt from show output would fail.

IMO there should be two things:
1. set_base_prompt() which updates self.router_prompt (maybe rename this self.base_prompt) which should be called as little as possible.  It would be called on initial login and maybe on enter/exit enable mode, maybe on enter/exit config mode (if necessary--i.e. the prompt changes sufficiently to require this).

2. find_prompt() which just returns the current router prompt (i.e. sends a blank '\n' down the channel and returns whatever comes back i.e. the current prompt).  Nothing is saved in the object because of this call.

Hey Pierre, thanks for pushing these, that's great.  Couple things:

1. Sorry, what was the parse bug?  Was the source context manager not working for you?  Worked in my tests.  It's pretty trivial & we don't really need it here, but if we drop it we should probably make sure to explicitly close() the source.

2. I don't think we should hard-code an SQL schema in models.py.  Really the schema definition belongs in database.py, and it should be configurable by the user with None as the default.

From the [SQLAlchemy docs](http://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/latest/core/metadata.html#sqlalchemy.schema.Table): 

> schema –
> The schema name for this table, which is required if the table resides in a schema other than the default selected schema for the engine’s database connection. Defaults to None.
> If the owning MetaData of this Table specifies its own MetaData.schema parameter, then that schema name will be applied to this Table if the schema parameter here is set to None.

So what's needed is to set ofxalchemy.database.Base.metadata inside init_db.  Something like this:
```
def init_db(db_uri, schema=None, **kwargs):
    engine = create_engine(db_uri, **kwargs)
    Base.metadata.schema = schema
    Base.metadata.create_all(bind=engine)
    Session.configure(bind=engine)
    return engine
```
Then when you're setting up your application you'd call:
`engine = ofxalchemy.database.init_db(URI, 'ofx')`

Would you do me a favor and see if that works for you?  It's much cleaner.

The issue here is that the API requests are paused internally during metadata updates. Producer.send() does not consider this a failure and so does not count it as a retry. A metadata update is triggered automatically on the first 'send' to a topic.

Until I executed it, I wouldn't have believed that this wouldn't have raised an error. I could have sworn that we used to raise an exception on extra attributes. But no, looking at the `Model.__init__` code, there isn't even a check for extra attributes. We just drop them on the ground if they are passed.

This exception is not surprising, given the previous non-exception, and that `foo` isn't supposed to be there.

Yep. Non-values are encoded as `None` in the dict, and are not passed into Redis (generally).

>

This returns a `False` value for intkey, which is weird. Should be `None`.

> So it seems exceptions in general are handled pretty well. My question is whether you should also add another one if the user tries to create an instance with an attribute that is not a member of the object class.

Yeah, an exception should be raised. Not having it raise an exception, but also not storing it violates the Python zens of "Errors should never pass silently." and "Unless explicitly silenced." I think this was meant to handle the case of data already in Redis where you are overlaying rom on top. I'll handle that with an explicit check for whether this is a new object, or being loaded, and an optional flag of `_extra_ok`, also in the exception message.

While looking into this, I also noticed that some weird session stuff was going on since I fixed bugs #108 and #109, so I've also got a fix for that too. Thank you for getting me to look into this.

> I also have another question. I noticed that in Redis you are using a hash table structure to create an index for each unique class attribute and you store there all the unique values. Is that more efficient than writing string value keys ? Would you think SETNX could possibly make a difference in performance here ?

Generally, yes, it is more efficient. Whatever inefficiencies you lose on by having another hash table (if it gets big), you more than make up for by removing the `<TableName>:<ColumnName>:uidx:` prefix that would be necessary if it was all in the main keyspace. So call it at least a 16 byte/entry savings, at minimum. For small unique indexes, all of the data is stored packed in a small string in Redis, so you do even better.

Also, Lua's a big part of how/why rom works, and we've got to make a trip through Lua in Redis. Using `SETNX` doesn't do us any good, unless we are trying to avoid Lua, explicitly avoid using Redis' rich types, etc. And ... that is not a design goal of rom; the "Redis Object Mapper".

> The reason I am asking this is because in TRIADB, value domain of primitive data types is separated and differentiated from the set of unique references to values that the type of a class attribute takes. Everything, including the Hypergraph mechanism, is based on single instances.

We improved item deletion in #117 (removing data can't induce a unique key violation / data error if you don't allow models with foreign key relationships), but item creation may be a bit funky (and may require not having foreign key relationships). I think I've got most of the details reasonably worked out in my head, but I'll have to think it over for a while before I move forward with my fix.

Speaking of which... the stuff I mentioned above as being broken/weird is fixed in 0.42.0, which I just released.

Yes, your python set cache might be useful if its size is not too big, otherwise membership check will become expensive. My previous benchmarks were based on TRIADB `add_datum (value)`, `add_item`, `add_row` logic with only `get_by` lookup but for bulk insertion it seems a columnar approach can be proven to be advantageous. For example index selectivity is an important parameter and the execution has to be planned and optimized accordingly. I have to run more tests to measure that.

import time

from PyQt5.QtCore import QThread


class Worker(QThread):

    def __init__(self):
        QThread.__init__(self)
        self.counter = 0

    def run(self):
        while not self.isInterruptionRequested():
            self.calculate()
            self.msleep(33) # 30Hz

    def calculate(self):

def main():

    w.start()
    assert w.isRunning()

    w.requestInterruption()
    w.wait()
    assert w.isFinished()

According to the data sheet, pins 19 and 20 configure the MPU interface.  I have my display configured 4-wire serial interface.  I'm assuming your using 3-wire which I believe won't work.  3-wire mode doesn't use DC signal; however, this signal is needed since the SSD 1322 driver sends both command and data packets.  Page 5 of the data sheet explains the MPU interface selections.

Change the width argument to 256. I know the NHD-2.7-12864WDY3 is 128x64; however, the SSD1322 driver is designed for 256x64.  Newhaven changed from SSD1325 (designed for 128x64) to SSD1322 for their NHD-2.7-12864xxxx products around June of 2017.  Essentially they are using a 256x64 controller for  a 128x64 display.   Using a width of 128 will make your images look like this:

![40576693-43a6ccac-60c0-11e8-82d7-dbd3fceb7e1f](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/19612797/41675331-0d84fa6e-7488-11e8-81ab-fbc3510ac59e.png)

But there wouldn't be a way to do that over any time period other than the most recent.  

Let me explain.  Every data point in `nflgame` today can be obtained easily from the web.  `nflgame` itself does not keep a historical record of every game other than keeping a copy of the .json file that feeds the gamecenter page.  ie. the Bills played the Titans in week 0 of the 2009 preseason.  The record of that game is still freely available [here](http://www.nfl.com/gamecenter/2009080950/2009/PRE0/bills@titans#menu=gameinfo%7CcontentId%3A09000d5d811ca252&tab=recap) and the .json file that fed that game could be easily downloaded (though for convenience and to save every user of `nflgame` from having to download all 1700+ games from 2009 to present it is already stored in `nflgame` in [/nflgame/gamecenter-json/2009080950.json.gz](https://github.com/BurntSushi/nflgame/blob/master/nflgame/gamecenter-json/2009080950.json.gz))  

`nflgame` didn't need to capture those games as they happened to have the data; the data is still present online today.

For the depth chart rank data you are talking about to have any meaning, one would need to know what the depth chart looked like during any given week.  A 3rd string RB today could easily have been a 1st string RB a few years ago.  Viewing his stats as a 3rd stringer for a game that he started and got the bulk of the carries in would not be an accurate portrayal of his performance against other 3rd stringers.  

Unfortunately, unless there is some other source of the data that hasn't yet been presented here, there just isn't a place to find who made up the depth chart for the Bills/Titans game in 2009.

I know this is an old issue but I found the source of the problem.  This is sort of unrelated to nflgame as I found the same issue with other scrapers and it was due to my poor wifi connection and lost packets.  The solution was to retry all get requests if the first one fails.  For a code sample I replace all

```
req = requests.get(url)
```
with
```
''' Get a url and return the request, try it up to 3 times if it fails initially'''
def retry_request(url):
    session = requests.Session()
    session.mount("http://", requests.adapters.HTTPAdapter(max_retries=3))
    return session.get(url=url)

req = retry_request(url)
```

my_env = {"TESSDATA_PREFIX":"/opt/local/share"}

(for me the /opt/local/share is the parent folder that contains tessdata, plz change accordingly)

Then in the same function change the "proc=..." to

    proc = subprocess.Popen(command, env=my_env,
            stderr=subprocess.PIPE)


Lastly, at the beginning of the file, change the definition of tesseract_cmd to:

tesseract_cmd = '/opt/local/bin/tesseract'

(namely to specify the absolute path, if you aren't sure, can go to shell and enter "which tesseract" to find out).

Thanks for the pull request! Yeah, figuring out how to parse footnotes took me a while. I should add some documentation at a certain point.

I think the important thing is that all `BlockToken` subclasses match *by line* rather than by character. All `BlockToken.read` methods assume they are taking an argument `lines`, which is an iterator containing the remaining lines with their respective newlines at the end.

What complicates `Footnote` is that whether a line is a part of a footnote reference cannot be decided from only the current line. Instead, we first join all the lines before an empty line:

```python
line_buffer = []
next_line = lines.peek()
while next_line is not None and next_line.strip() != '':
    line_buffer.append(next(lines))
    next_line = lines.peek()
string = ''.join(line_buffer)
```

... and then do our matches on the concatenated string (with newlines in them). When we encounter anything that does not form a footnote reference, we need to "give back" the number of lines still unmatched in the concatenated string, because they are probably part of another block-level token. This is why every time before we return `None` in `Footnote.match_reference`, we need to make a call to `Footnote.backtrack`. The number of lines we need to give back is equal to the number of newlines remaining, so this is where `lines._index -= string[offset+1:].count('\n')` comes from.

More related to your question: are you passing a list of lines without newlines into mistletoe? The assumption that block-level tokens can match on a list of newline-terminating lines is quite entrenched at this point (which is why, for example, [this line](https://github.com/miyuchina/mistletoe/blob/7e28934d4f089ebf8b494ba26aff71947bed83f7/mistletoe/block_token.py#L144) exists). My reasoning is that most people will be using a Markdown parser like this:

```python
with open('foo.md', 'r') as fin:
    return markdown(fin)
```

... and `fin` will have all the newlines we need. Does this apply to your scenario? Would you prefer a built-in mechanism to append newlines?

You have alerted me to another edge case, however, where a document does not terminate with newlines. I'll open another issue for that!

Let me know what you think. Thanks again!

We have a document where we use all sorts of different labels for equations. For example, sometimes we label one equation (CO2.1), and then later label another version of that equation as (CO2.1'). I'm open to changing things, but from @svisser's comment I think we might not be on the same page re: what this method does (or should do). This is certainly at least partially my fault for not being more thorough in the PR description (and perhaps in the docstring as well).

The way the method works in the current implementation isn't that `"male"` gets converted to `"male"`; rather, it get converted to `str`. The use case I have in mind is that you have a known-good object (what is sometimes called "golden data")—perhaps already available in a handy JSON file—and you want to create a schema to validate objects that look *like* that object, i.e. have the same keys, with values of the same types.

So:

```python
s = Schema.infer({
    'int': 42,
    'str': 'foo',
    'bool': True
})

s({'int': 27, 'str': 'bar', 'bool': False})  # valid
```

I don't personally think it's necessary (or even feasible) for this method to provide a comprehensive way of inferring every feature already supported by the `Schema` class. It seems the scenario @svisser had in mind was the case of _enums_ (`["male", "female", "other"]`); I am personally fine with a limitation of this method being that you can't infer those, or ranges, or callables, or any number of other things. But it *does* provide a very easy way of, for example, quickly scaffolding a suite of API regression tests.

We need to stop the bikeshedding.

The more I hear Hynek's enthusiasm for his naming of attr.s the less I trust his instincts about naming.

I think dataclasses is still the best name I've heard so far, so let's please stick with that. The argument against it seems to basically boil down to "it makes you think it's a special kind of class" which, actually, I think is totally okay -- dataclasses have a secret handshake (`__dataclasses_fields__`) and automatically generate a constructor that makes it easy to construct an instance from its field values, while the generated `__repr__` shows all those fields (etc.).

TBH I'm not sure I'll use dataclasses a lot myself -- my classes usually have a lot more state than I want to pass in to the constructor, and I often have a custom repr() that compactly shows the most important state I care about in a typical debugging session. I also don't feel I am reluctant to create small classes when they fit in the design.

I've realised that this wrapper was responsible for my erroneous issue #41. The problem is that `readlink -e` dereferences symlinks. Instead, you can use `realpath -s`, which resolves mounts but not symlinks. i.e. the full command is

    #!/usr/bin/env bash
    # Workaround for trash-cli not being able to trash files on the *same* partition. https://github.com/andreafrancia/trash-cli/issues/38
    
    for i in "$@"; do
      fullpath="$(realpath -s "$i")"
      fixedpath="$(echo "$fullpath" | sed "s,/HDD/$USER,$HOME/HDD,")"
      /usr/bin/trash "$fixedpath"
    done

We should now have full read-write recurrence support. There's support for creating daily, weekly, monthly and yearly recurrences (the latter two in relative and absolute versions). Everything is implemented in https://github.com/ecederstrand/exchangelib/blob/master/exchangelib/recurrence.py

Here's an example of creating 7 occurrences on Mondays and Wednesdays of every third week, starting September 1, 2017:

```python
from exchangelib import CalendarItem, EWSDateTime, EWSTimeZone, EWSDate
from exchangelib.recurrence import Recurrence, WeeklyPattern, MONDAY, WEDNESDAY

item = CalendarItem(
    folder=a.calendar,
    start=tz.localize(EWSDateTime(2017, 9, 1, 11)),
    end=tz.localize(EWSDateTime(2017, 9, 1, 13)),
    subject='Hello Recurrence',
    recurrence=Recurrence(pattern=WeeklyPattern(interval=3, weekdays=[MONDAY, WEDNESDAY]), start=EWSDate(2017, 9, 1), number=7),
)

for i in a.calendar.filter(start__lt=end, end__gt=start):
    print(i.subject, i.start, i.end)
    print(i.recurrence)
    print(i.first_occurrence)
    print(i.last_occurrence)
    for o in i.modified_occurrences:
        print(o)
    for x in i.deleted_occurrences:
        print(o)
```

Maybe this would help explain my use case scenario.  In order for me to get to the FrankNetwork, I need to first go through one of the gateway systems (requiring 2-factor auth).  From there, I can go to Frankbastion and then finally into the Franknetwork.  Similar rules apply for BeanNetwork, etc.


                      +------------+
     o                |            |  ->  Frankbastion  ->  FrankNetwork
    /|\       ->      |  Gateways  |       
    / \               |            |  ->  Beanbastion   ->  BeanNetwork
         ControlPath  +------------+

To take advantage of only having to 2-factor authenticate once for the gateways, I setup a ControlPath only to them and then have DynamicForward for bastion hosts.  I'd assumed that I've written the assh.yml correctly because it seems to do what I want -- except for the fact the a temporary directory is getting created even if ControlPath is defined and getting used.

Seems that because I don't have a ControlPath defined for Frankbastion or Beansbastion, assh is automatically creating a temp directory for their defined gateways -- even though the gateway has a ControlPath defined.

I've also noted that setting ControlMaster no / ControlPath none in the defaults won't prevent the creation of the temp directory.  So... let's say I do `ssh 172.31.29.255/172.16.254.0`, a directory called `172.16.254.0` gets created in the current working directory.

All that said, I really appreciate you adding the ability to disable the auto-creation of directories.

You can already try using:

```ini
[default]
Includes = ~/.ssh/host/host1/config.advanced ~/.ssh/host/host2/config.advanced ~/.ssh/host/host3/config.advanced
User = $USER
Port = 22
PubkeyAuthentication = yes
IdentityFile = ~/.ssh/id_rsa
ProxyCommand = assh connect %h --port=%p
```

The `Includes` directive is inside the `[default]` section, but it accepts new host `sections`, I will try to allow the `Includes` directive to be used outside of the `[default]` section, so it will be less confusing

I will implement globing to support `~/.ssh/host/**/config.advanced`

That's an interesting use case. So basically the source consists of multiple zstandard frames without any extra "framing" indicating where each zstandard frame begins and ends. As a result, you want an API where you can pass data into the decompressor incrementally and then know where in the input stream you left off so you can start a fresh decompression operation at the beginning of the next frame.

This seems like a legitimate use case. I also consider it an API bug in python-zstandard that it doesn't expose when an input was partially consumed nor whether a frame boundary occurred.

We can't change the API of `decompressobj.decompress()` because it needs to conform to the API of Python's standard library modules. But we could expose a `decompress2()` (or similar) that allows the caller to know about offsets and/or frame boundaries.

I'll look at incorporating your patch (or a variation thereof) next time I sit down to hack on python-zstandard. I'm not sure when that will be. If it is a high priority for you, let me know.

First you need an array indicating the vertices that need to be visible and the array size for the vertices in each frame needs to be the same even though they are not visible.

Then consider the situation where you have a mesh which starts with a mesh of lets say 500 vertices and is growing over time to substantial vertex numbers let say a million. This will result in vertex attribute arrays with a million elements for a frame with only 500 visible vertices.

I think a choice of either dynamic vertices and triangles or interpolation worth be a way to go now at least that is what I am trying at the moment. But to do it in the same MeshView would be a bit messy cause the interpolation is really baked-in the MeshView WidgetView, maybe a separate WidgetView?

You can even hide the separation of the Dynamic Vertex MeshView and the standard MeshView by looking at the sizes of the vertex arrays in ipyvolume and then selecting the right meshview based on that.

Sorry for the ramble, just brain storming here.

I ran into the same issue yesterday. I tracked it down to the way validation is handled in `BulkUpdateModelMixin`. In `bulk_update()` a [serializer is created](https://github.com/miki725/django-rest-framework-bulk/blob/615393d/rest_framework_bulk/drf2/mixins.py#L73-L78) with the QuerySet for the view, and then validated. The [validation](https://github.com/tomchristie/django-rest-framework/blob/2.4.8/rest_framework/serializers.py#L528-L529) in `BaseSerializer` loops over the entire QuerySet to extract their identities, which can be very slow, especially if you have a lot of records.

I worked around the issue by adding an [extra filter](https://gist.github.com/hackedd/d97ca2f282b53c33f990) to the affected views, that checks if it looks like a bulk update is in process, and filters the QuerySet to only those items that are about to be updated.

Hi @MelissaLAC , 

Thank you for your comment. You are right, we are using exactly the opposite notation from Steck for two forms of reduced dipole matrix elements (one being symmetric upon change of order of j1 and j2, and the other one not). There is unfortunately no consistent notation on this in the literature if you look at angular momentum algebra books from different authors. That's why Atom calculator returns numerical values for both so that people can easily spot if their preferred notation matches with our or not. 

We've chosen to use < j1 || ... || j2 > for the reduced d.m.e. that has the same value when you change order of j1 and j2 because bra ket notation looks like you are taking matrix element, and matrix elements should be just one complex conjugate different. This reserves ( j1 || ... || j2 ) notation for reduced d.m.e whose numerical value is not equal to ( j2 || ... || j1 ) in general. This is opposite to Steck notation. 

There is a note on this in ARC documentation but it is good to have this noted here too as it often causes confusion. Thank you for your question and please let me know if something is unclear! 

For your project, we have some ARC black body examples here . Good luck! 

Best wishes, Nikola

One thing to think about is that Migrate restoring behavior from 1.6.4 (or older) may make it harder for people to update from newer versions. If someone just wanted to aid the upgrade process from 1.8 to 3.0.0 Compat then restoring the old `.attr`/`.prop` semantics is only going to hurt.

If Migrate continued supporting older versions, one way to upgrade would be to not change the `.attr`/`.prop` behavior but to warn if used in a probably incorrect way so that people can:

1. Use an old jQuery.
2. Add a Migrate to it, fix warnings.
3. Update jQuery, fix warnings.
4. Remove Migrate.

Now the process will have to look like:

1. Use an old jQuery.
2. Update jQuery & add a Migrate to it, fix warnings.
3. Remove Migrate.

We need to think how to accommodate both people updating from 1.6.4 and ones updating from 1.9.

Ok. How about this solution:

```python
class TaskProcess(multiprocessing.Process):

    def __init__(self, ..., tracking_url_callback=None, status_message_callback=None):
        ...
        self.tracking_url_callback = tracking_url_callback
        self.status_message_callback = status_message_callback
        ...

    def _run_get_new_deps(self):
        self.task.set_tracking_url = self.tracking_url_callback
        self.task.set_status_message = self.status_message_callback

        def deprecated_tracking_url_callback(*args, **kwargs):
            warnings.warn("tracking_url_callback in run() args is deprecated, use "
                          "set_tracking_url instead.", DeprecationWarning)
            self.tracking_url_callback(*args, **kwargs)

        run_again = False
        try:
            task_gen = self.task.run(tracking_url_callback=deprecated_tracking_url_callback)
        except TypeError as ex:
            if 'unexpected keyword argument' not in str(ex):
                raise
            run_again = True
        if run_again:
            task_gen = self.task.run()

        self.task.set_tracking_url = None
        self.task.set_status_message = None
        ...
```

The callbacks are set right before run() is called, and then reset. This way pickling is not affected, it's backward compatible, and at some point the try-except block can be refactored.

The issue is process.extract runs using fuzz.WRatio. In turn WRatio runs utils.full_process on both query and on the items in collection which turns your unicode string into an empty string. By design empty string comparisons return 0.

```
In[2]: from fuzzywuzzy import process, fuzz, utils
In[3]: utils.full_process(u'⊂ ⊃ ⊆ ⊇ ⊈ ⊉ ⊊ ⊋ ⊄ ⊅ ⫅ ⫆ ⫋ ⫌ ⫃ ⫄ ⫇ ⫈ ⫉ ⫊ ⟃ ⟄')
Out[3]: 
''
```

Interestingly the first item in your list is the only item that full_process does not turn into the empty string. This leads to an interesting issue if you use a different scorer.

```
In[15]: process.extract(string, collection, scorer=fuzz.ratio, limit=10)
Out[15]: 

[('¶ ∑ ∞ ∫ π ∈ ℝ² ∖ ⩆ ⩇ ⩈ ⩉ ⩊ ⩋ ⪽ ⪾ ⪿ ⫀ ⫁ ⫂ ⋒ ⋓', 12),
 ('⊂ ⊃ ⊆ ⊇ ⊈ ⊉ ⊊ ⊋ ⊄ ⊅ ⫅ ⫆ ⫋ ⫌ ⫃ ⫄ ⫇ ⫈ ⫉ ⫊ ⟃ ⟄', 0),
 ('∠ ∡ ⦛ ⦞ ⦟ ⦢ ⦣ ⦤ ⦥ ⦦ ⦧ ⦨ ⦩ ⦪ ⦫ ⦬ ⦭ ⦮ ⦯ ⦓ ⦔ ⦕ ⦖ ⟀', 0),
 ('∫ ∬ ∭ ∮ ∯ ∰ ∱ ∲ ∳ ⨋ ⨌ ⨍ ⨎ ⨏ ⨐ ⨑ ⨒ ⨓ ⨔ ⨕ ⨖ ⨗ ⨘ ⨙ ⨚ ⨛ ⨜', 0),
 ('≁ ≂ ≃ ≄ ⋍ ≅ ≆ ≇ ≈ ≉ ≊ ≋ ≌ ⩯ ⩰ ⫏ ⫐ ⫑ ⫒ ⫓ ⫔ ⫕ ⫖', 0),
 ('¬ ⫬ ⫭ ⊨ ⊭ ∀ ∁ ∃ ∄ ∴ ∵ ⊦ ⊬ ⊧ ⊩ ⊮ ⊫ ⊯ ⊪ ⊰ ⊱ ⫗ ⫘', 0),
 ('∧ ∨ ⊻ ⊼ ⊽ ⋎ ⋏ ⟑ ⟇ ⩑ ⩒ ⩓ ⩔ ⩕ ⩖ ⩗ ⩘ ⩙ ⩚ ⩛ ⩜ ⩝ ⩞ ⩟ ⩠ ⩢', 0)]
```

This occurs because process.extract is still running this full_process on the 'choices' but also it's not running it on the 'query' (I think this is a bug and will submit it shortly).

 ('¬ ⫬ ⫭ ⊨ ⊭ ∀ ∁ ∃ ∄ ∴ ∵ ⊦ ⊬ ⊧ ⊩ ⊮ ⊫ ⊯ ⊪ ⊰ ⊱ ⫗ ⫘', 48),

It sounds like what you're talking about is `pip install -e`, which under the hood just invokes `setup.py develop` which itself invokes `easy_install`.

Since this issue only affects `develop` installs and not normal installs, it seems largely mitigated by the recommendation to primarily use `pip install`.

I'd like to focus the remaining energy we have onto some of the larger goals, such as removing easy_install. One step that will have to take place to remove easy_install is to solve the `setup.py develop`/`pip install -e` use case, preferably in a library that pip or setuptools can invoke (probably packaging+distlib, but maybe just distlib). Solving that would reduce pip's dependency on setuptools but will also solve this issue.

>  I don't understand why allocating the variable so you can mutate is any worse (and potentially so much better performance wise)?

Consider this example:

```python
class MutableObject(object):
  __slots__ = ("foo", "bar",)
  def __init__(self, foo, bar):
    self.foo = foo
    self.bar = bar

  def mutate(self, foo, bar):
    self.foo = foo
    self.bar = bar
```

`obj = MutableObject("Foo", "Bar")` translates to the following python byte code:

```
  1           0 LOAD_NAME                0 (MutableObject)
              3 LOAD_CONST               0 ('Foo')
              6 LOAD_CONST               1 ('Bar')
              9 CALL_FUNCTION            2
             12 STORE_NAME               1 (obj)
             15 LOAD_CONST               2 (None)
             18 RETURN_VALUE        
None
```

Meanwhile, `obj.mutate("FOO", "BAR")`, which mutates the existing instance, translates to this:

```
  1           0 LOAD_NAME                0 (obj)
              3 LOAD_ATTR                1 (mutate)
              6 LOAD_CONST               0 ('FOO')
              9 LOAD_CONST               1 ('BAR')
             12 CALL_FUNCTION            2
             15 POP_TOP             
             16 LOAD_CONST               2 (None)
             19 RETURN_VALUE        
None
```

Alternatively, if you were to access the attrs directly and assign values externally (e.g. `obj.foo = "FOO"; obj.bar = "BAR"`):

```
  1           0 LOAD_CONST               0 ('FOO')
              3 LOAD_NAME                0 (obj)
              6 STORE_ATTR               1 (foo)
              9 LOAD_CONST               1 ('BAR')
             12 LOAD_NAME                0 (obj)
             15 STORE_ATTR               2 (bar)
             18 LOAD_CONST               2 (None)
             21 RETURN_VALUE        
None
```

Then, compare the bytecode for `MutableObject.__init__()`:

```
Austins-MacBook-Pro:Desktop amarshall$ python mutant2.py 
  6           0 LOAD_FAST                1 (foo)
              3 LOAD_FAST                0 (self)
              6 STORE_ATTR               0 (foo)

  7           9 LOAD_FAST                2 (bar)
             12 LOAD_FAST                0 (self)
             15 STORE_ATTR               1 (bar)
             18 LOAD_CONST               0 (None)
             21 RETURN_VALUE        
None
```

To `obj.mutate()`:

```
 10           0 LOAD_FAST                1 (foo)
              3 LOAD_FAST                0 (self)
              6 STORE_ATTR               0 (foo)

 11           9 LOAD_FAST                2 (bar)
             12 LOAD_FAST                0 (self)
             15 STORE_ATTR               1 (bar)
             18 LOAD_CONST               0 (None)
             21 RETURN_VALUE        
None
```

Notice, there's effectively no difference between the bytecode for the `__init__` and `mutate` functions.

The only _real_ difference, complexity-wise between the two are the extra `LOAD_ATTR` and `POP_TOP` in `obj.mutate("FOO", "BAR")`.  If anything, the mutation should be only very slightly slow, if noticeable at all.  Having to pull the item out of cache would slow things down even more.

querystring, headers, body, path is the location of where cornice will check for the data.

- querystring is what's in the querystring (e.g after the `?` in the URL)
- headers is http headers
- body is the body of the request (for POST, PUT, DELETE)
- path is what's in the path part of the URL (depending the variables you defined in your URL schemes).

Another thing in the same area of code. Situation - 3 node cluster (A,B,C), Patroni is paused, there is a manual failover key for A->B, leader A has just demoted itself. B and C are racing to run run_cycle.

1. If B runs before C, then B acquires leader lock, promoets itself. C does not set the rewind flag, starts following B.
2. If C runs before B, then C sets the rewind flag, follow() return 'no action'. Now B runs. C runs next cycle, then next cycle C will do a rewind and start following B.
3. LIke case 2, but B does not manage to run between the two C cycles. Now C will call Postgresql.follow, where the rewind flag is cleared because there is no leader, and C is restarted as a standby without primary_conninfo. When B now runs, C will not rewind from it.

That seems somewhat inconsistent. Why is the rewind flag being cleared if follow gets called without a leader?

For example, if `fdisk` program is running inside shell, typing some symbols can lead to really unexpected results. `innotop` connected to production database is also not a good place to carelessly type something :)

The safest option is to check if `pgrep -P <shell_pid>` is empty, and shell have no child processes. But this prevents us from running command in shells with background tasks. To allow command inside shell with background tasks, we should check if all processes from `pgrep -P <shell_pid>` are is stopped (T) state.

The file `diffexp_dexseq.tab.gz` contains floats. Floating-point numbers are not guaranteed to be returned in exactly the same form on different systems. To test process output field values we use [`assertAlmostEqualGeneric`](https://github.com/genialis/resolwe/blob/master/resolwe/test/testcases/__init__.py#L93). However, we don't have an equivalent solution developed for files yet. - `.thing()` should never have required parameters

from google.protobuf.json_format import MessageToJson
serialized = MessageToJson(original)

tbh I actually wanted to suggest dropping 3.3 since -- as you mentioned -- Python 3.3 [EOL](https://docs.python.org/devguide/#status-of-python-branches) approaches and pip [has already dropped support for 3.3](https://github.com/pypa/pip/issues/3796). I know that steps like these usually cause an uproar in the community. But a) it seems the amount of people still using 3.3 is rather low (see the pip issue) which b) may not justify the increased maintenance effort necessary caused by covering all the corner cases and increased code clutter. You can see from the traceback and the `T4` in the `dill` trace tells me that `dill` is passing off the pickling of the `Session` class to `pickle` with `save_global`.  For most classes, `pickle` can do the job, but apparently this is one of those cases where `pickle` fails on a user-built class.  Since the return of the `Session` object is a problem, could you not just store it as `self.session`, or in a dict of `self.sessions`, and then return `None` or a string for the key for the session?

Another workaround would be to add a `__reduce__` method to your class, or to equivalently teach pickle how to reduce your class with `copy_reg`.

Workarounds aside, this seems to be a corner case that, as far as I can tell, is due to your class construction more than the inability to serialize a `Session` object -- which `dill` should handle when asked directly (demonstrated previously).  So what I'd say here, is that unless you can distill the example down to something that demonstrates it's a more general pickling failure, it's not something that `dill` should handle in it's code base.   What I mean is that if you can construct a minimal example that can help demonstrate it a broader type of failure (e.g. a programming pattern that fails for all classes, or it's **the** primary use case in `sqlalchemy`, or something like that), then one of my suggestions above is probably the best path forward.

The reason you can't import Bootstrap after installing it with NPM is because Sass doesn't know that module exists. You need to either import Bootstrap using a relative path to your node_modules folder (bad) or use (Eyeglass)[https://github.com/sass-eyeglass/eyeglass] to give Sass the ability to use Bootstrap as a node module. Eyeglass is not yet supported by Bootstrap. You can look at my pull request to see how I have added Eyeglass support and how you can import parts or all of Bootstrap in a Sass project that uses Eyeglass.

Yes, I plan to update my stack on every deploy. That way, on every deploy I keep the same ELB with the same ASG, but a new LaunchConfiguration (LC) is created. This LC name contains the version that I'm deploying together with the current timestamp. When the deploy is made, since the LC is new, a rolling update is done and all the instances in the ASG get replaced by instances for the new LC.

Keeping one LC for every deploy allows me to rollback whenever I want and keep track of all the deploys.

I think --inherit-path is broken conceptually too.  It should probably take an argument that is a whitelisted requirement, i.e. '--inherit-path scikit-learn --inherit-path numpy==1.9.1'.  As a global 'inherit anything from system packages' option, we lose the ability to control which version of a dep gets imported.  E.g. if the machine has thrift 0.5 and you bundle thrift 0.9 with your pex, even if we reorder sys.path, there may be path importer caching and module.__path__ stuff laying around from evaluating .pth files in site-packages on start-up that causes 0.5 to get imported when you do 'import thrift.'

FYI I did this in 3 steps:

1) Encrypt the key using aws cli: 
```aws kms encrypt --key-id KMS_KEY_ID --plaintext THE_SECRET_TOKEN```

2) Include the encrypted token inside my SAM yaml:
```
Environment:
        Variables:
          SECRET_TOKEN: '...524krvmX9JA='
```
3) Within the lambda, decrypt the ENV. Ex using Python
```
import boto3
import os
from base64 import b64decode

ENCRYPTED = os.environ['SECRET_TOKEN']
DECRYPTED = boto3.client('kms').decrypt(CiphertextBlob=b64decode(ENCRYPTED))['Plaintext'].decode('utf-8')
```

With this approach, I can check in the encrypted key inside git and I don't have to enter the key from UI Console manually.

Hi All, I know it's closed, but I have just been making use of this - so thanks for the work you both put into this.  As I need to use pretty basic Python (No binaries), I couldn't use "netifaces" so in case it's of any help to anyone else, I ended up doing the following:

```
    # Use the default method to search for Sonos Devices
    try:
        sonos_devices = soco.discover(timeout=5)
    except:
        log("SonosDiscovery: Exception when getting devices")
        log("SonosDiscovery: %s" % traceback.format_exc())
        sonos_devices = []

    # If there are multiple network devices, then this may not have picked up
    # the correct network device to broadcast on, so try another way
    if (sonos_devices is None) or (len(sonos_devices) < 1):
        try:
            # Try and find the address ourselves
            interfaceAddr = socket.gethostbyname(socket.gethostname())
            log("SonosDiscovery: Searching for devices using gethostname %s" % interfaceAddr)
            if interfaceAddr not in [None, '']:
                sonos_devices = soco.discover(timeout=5, interface_addr=interfaceAddr)
        except:
            log("SonosDiscovery: Exception when getting devices")
            log("SonosDiscovery: %s" % traceback.format_exc())
            sonos_devices = []

    # If still not found, try yet another method
    if (sonos_devices is None) or (len(sonos_devices) < 1):
        try:
            # Try and find the address ourselves
            interfaceAddr = socket.gethostbyname(socket.getfqdn())
            log("SonosDiscovery: Searching for devices using getfqdn %s" % interfaceAddr)
            if interfaceAddr not in [None, '']:
                sonos_devices = soco.discover(timeout=5, interface_addr=interfaceAddr)
        except:
            log("SonosDiscovery: Exception when getting devices")
            log("SonosDiscovery: %s" % traceback.format_exc())
            sonos_devices = []

    # If still not found, try the last option
    if (sonos_devices is None) or (len(sonos_devices) < 1):
        try:
            # Try and find the address ourselves by going to a web page
            s = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_DGRAM)
            s.connect(("sonos.com",80))
            interfaceAddr = s.getsockname()[0]
            s.close()
            log("SonosDiscovery: Searching for devices using web search %s" % interfaceAddr)
            if interfaceAddr not in [None, '']:
                sonos_devices = soco.discover(timeout=5, interface_addr=interfaceAddr)
        except:
            log("SonosDiscovery: Exception when getting devices")
            log("SonosDiscovery: %s" % traceback.format_exc())
            sonos_devices = []
```

@khangmach 
There are a good number of edge cases here.

Each facility has it's own config. One of them might have a "simple" sign in with just username. If the default facility doesn't, the password gets shown. In this case, a user might not know how to sign in with the password field there.

Also, we don't currently check for collisions on the background, the facility is specified in the frontend, currently just using the backend's default. 

Initially, I was thinking we could just display this modal whenever there's an error on default signin (so users are given the option to sign in to the proper facility). The issues with this approach is that a single user with the same username/password in 2 places wouldn't ever see it.

Here's the current approach I'm considering, if we dont' want to have a `<select>` on the sign in page:
1. Check all facilities on the device. If any one of them has simple signin, hide the password field.
2. Have the user type in their username, then submit the form
3. Display modal, allow them to select the facility
4. If the facility has simple sign in, sign them in immediately. Otherwise, throw them back to signin page with the password field highlighted.

I would have given the above some gherkin stories, but I'm losing a good amount of time on this bug (Lessons pages still need some stubbing for tomorrow + there's a good amount of work left).

I’m looking at the Digi docs for ZigBee here http://ftp1.digi.com/support/documentation/90002002.pdf  and comparing them to the Digimesh version here: http://www.digi.com/resources/documentation/digidocs/pdfs/90000991.pdf

I’ve checked the ZigBee document again and realised I missed the `0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF` 64-bit unknown address mentioned in your link because it doesn’t appear in the API Frame specification section for the ZigBee Transmit and Explicit Addressing ZigBee Command Frame (pages 148 and 149) but now that I’m looking for it, I see that it is mentioned elsewhere in that document.

Now that I’ve seen it, I think `0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF` (Unknown address) is the appropriate equivalent default for the 64-bit addresses, equivalent to the 16-bit Unknown address `0xFFFE`. That should allow ZIgBee users to just set the address version they want to use, and the other will not interfere. Digimesh users (like me) can safely ignore the 16-bit address.

The current 64-bit default for `remote_at` `dest_addr_long` did seem odd to me, according to the docs above it’s the ‘Reserved 64-bit address for the coordinator’, but the part of the doc I was focused on implied that it was the only other ‘special’ 64-bit address other than broadcast (`0x000000000000FFFF`). There doesn't seem to be a reason from the spec to have that as a default as far as I can see.

I agree with you that the corresponding default 64-bit unknown address should be added. I’ll update the PR to try and keep everything consistent. Thanks for that link, I had a feeling I was missing something about the 64 bit addressing, which is why I initially left those alone. After comparing the documents, the addresses are clearer to me now.

To verify, the task you request is to change
<memento.us/>; rel="original",
in Link TimeMaps and
`!meta {"original_uri": "memento.us/"}`
in CDXJ TimeMaps to be `<http://memento.us/>; rel="original",` and `!meta {"original_uri": "http://memento.us/"}`, respectively?

I can foresee this breaking tools (e.g., Mink) that look to the URI-R to "jump back" to the live Web only to find that the URI-R was never served under that scheme (be it http or https).

I also don't think "//memento.us" is proper here, as it banks on the relativity of the contextual representation (i.e., the TimeMap) per RFC3896. Then again, per that same RFC, a URI in the context of the Link-formatted TimeMaps (whose fundamental syntax are defined in RFC8288) must include a scheme.  Thus, the URI-R in the Link-formatted TimeMap must have a scheme for the TimeMap to be Memento compliant.

So I think this issue is capturing a couple of different cases where 1.9-1.10 migrations are stuck in an intermediate state.

We switched the Builder's internal port from 22 to 2223 for Flannel compatibility. But this means that if the Fleet unit files get out of sync then Builder will be listening on one port and the Fleet units will set up the other port.

So a 1.9 release with a fleet units for 1.10 will have the problem that @sstarcher is reporting, and a 1.10 release with 1.9 units may hit a slightly different variation of the problem, where Fleet never returns because it's waiting for port 22 to come up, and the container is running on 2223.

Dropping an idea here.

The reason that we need the `copy()` command is that we can't rely on reverting all of the canonical based information from the database (number-to-canonical-hash, canonical-head, txn-to-canonical-block).  Also, we don't actually want to revert that chain information because we need both chains to have access to their *canonical-thing* database entries.

The other data is going to *normally* be safe to already have there as I don't believe that we have any code that does pre-checks before storing things like blocks, transactions, receipts, etc.

If we **could** do that, then it'd should be safe to re-use the same database for fork chains.  If we can do this then copying chains just got a lot easier.  So my thought was, maybe we can figure out a scheme where database re-use is safe because we have a reliable way of storing per-chain canonical block information with no chance of cross-leakage.

I've been thinking about different keying schemes, ideally something that lets us key the canonical data to a specific chain.  It could be something as simple as each chain having a unique `salt` that is part of the key we store canonical data under.  Since we already have a schema system for how those keys are generated this should be really simple to implement.

Yeah, the personal module doesn't get a lot of love, partially because it is not standard across geth and parity.

This might be one of those places where deviating from the web3.js implementation is a cleaner API and more pythonic. In your example, the hex-encoded string `'0x68656C6C6F'` does not seem like the most natural representation of the message that you're signing. The "truest" representation of the message is `bytes` and the fact that `'hello'` was supported as a string is an accidental side-effect of old py2 support code in eth-utils. So the best answer is that `b'hello'` is the message that you're signing.

My alternative proposed solution is to:

- [ ] Only accept `bytes` type in the message argument, rejecting `str` as a `TypeError` that guides how to convert text or hex to bytes
- [ ] Document `w3.personal.sign` (which appears to not be documented *at all* at the moment).
- [ ] Document examples of how to sign both text and binary messages, something like:

```py
from web3.auto import w3
w3.personal.sign('hello'.encode(), w3.eth.coinbase, 'account-password')

# or
msg = w3.toBytes(hexstr='0x68656C6C6F')
w3.personal.sign(msg, w3.eth.coinbase, 'account-password')
```

Another reasonable alternative is to set up `sign()` to take keyword args like this does: http://web3py.readthedocs.io/en/latest/web3.eth.account.html#web3.account.Account.sign

> You write "confirmed once" - what were the other outcomes?

The rpc result was `null`, so the python result was `None`. Even for a locally broadcasted transaction, this happened for a little while, but after a couple retries on the same hash, I eventually got the `blockHash=None` receipt.

> If the former, then Parity's behaviour is inconsistent, and it's worth reporting upstream.

It seems that the practice of returning a receipt with `blockHash=None` is necessarily inconsistent, since you might or might not have the transaction in your local transaction pool. If you don't then returning even a "partial receipt" is impossible.

Given Gavin's quote on that parity issue, it seems unlikely that parity is interested in moving toward the wiki-defined behavior:

> as a side note: historically, cpp-ethereum implemented this RPC like parity (and that predated the docs you quote). this is because it gives more information to the user, since you are able to see ahead of time which logs and gas usage the transaction had. geth apparently did it differently. geth's simpler behaviour made its way into the ethereum-foundation-hosted docs that you quote for no good reason.

To be fair, the RPC methods haven't actually gone through any EIP process. So despite being a bit cranky, he's right that it is a bit weak to lean on the wiki as "the standard". Yet another reason to formally define the json-rpc methods in an EIP.

```
    filters:

      direction: older

and adding an exclude filter solved it.

```
    filters:
    - filtertype: pattern
      kind: timestring
      value: '%Y.%m.%d'
      exclude: True
    - filtertype: age
      source: name
      direction: older
      timestring: '%Y.%W'
      unit: weeks

Two reasons for the original change:

1) Differentiate between:
a) The command successfully completed, but there was no matching resource.
b) The command failed for some reason - we don't know if there is a resource.
c) The command successfully completed, and there is a resource.

2) Enable users to use `set -e` in their bash scripts. 

There should not be any *parsing* of the output needed (unless checking for an empty string constitutes parsing). 

The only time where a command should return a failure code on exists is if an empty string does not uniquely indicate that the resource doesn't exists (consider a `blob content show` like command where it would be impossible to distinguish between an existing blob with empty content and a non-existent blob. If this is not happening, please open a bug. 

@colemickens, are you advocating for having a universal "exists" command? The only other alternative I see is to have different error codes for "doesn't exist" and "failed to determine if it exists" - with the downside of this being a breaking change (again) as well as breaking the ability to use `set -e` in many cases...

It turns out that Jupyter have connection between kernels on different ports (not 8888), so when heavy firewall installed, it blocks such connections as suspected danger and kernels don't receive messages between each other and so Jupyter doesn't work.
Simplest solution for this is running notebook on port 8889 or any other. That explains why running jupyter lab temporary solves the problem, as it runs on 8889 when 8888 is busy.

**Short answer: change port from 8888 to 8889**

I think we have to be really careful about presenting the user with too
many choices in dialogs that require them to read carefully. Especially
with destructive actions, it becomes more likely the user will
misunderstand the subtle differences and pick the wrong one.

Also, it is common for users to get into a workflow where they are doing
the different variants of restart often. We want these actions to be super
fast and automatic. In a given notebook, it is also likely that a user will
using the same restart logic repeatedly.

Based on these observations, here is a thought:

* Offer a single restart menu item and button.
* Bring up a modal dialog that has only two buttons (Restart, Cancel)
* Expose the different restart options (Clear Output, Run All) as
checkboxes in the dialog.
* And here is the *important* part - for each notebook sessions, remember
the user's last selection of those checkboxes.

Benefits:

* Easy/quick to scan the checkbox options visually.
* Those options are separate from the main choice to restart.
* Once a user picks the right options, they can restart extremely fast with
"00 return".

With the latest commits I think this PR has become mergeable.

In short:

* `DateTimeType` gets time zone support plus a built-in parser.
* `TimestampType` becomes more usable and bug-free.
* The `types.temporal` module is **removed**.

No external dependencies are needed. The only open question is what default behavior to choose w.r.t. timezones.

PR #310 added support for a UTC designator in the parsing format but did nothing to actually handle the time zone information. Going forward, there are several reasonable options:

* (A) Accept all kinds of inputs and attach `tzinfo` only if the input contains time zone information.

  * `2015-01-01 12:00:00-07:00` would be imported as `datetime(2015, 1, 1, 12, 0, tzinfo=offset_timezone(-07:00))`
  * Pros: Has the least surprising `to_native` outcome.
  * Cons: Resulting objects may be either naive or aware depending on input.

* (B) Accept all kinds of inputs. Convert timezone-aware values to UTC and drop the `tzinfo` objects.

  * `2015-01-01 12:00:00-07:00` would be imported as `datetime.datetime(2015, 1, 1, 19, 0)`
  * Pros: An optimized setup in terms of actual application design. Compatible; preserves current behavior for both presently accepted formats.
  * Cons: Opinionated. Not viable for fields that must be able to accept both local times and timezone-aware values, but I believe this to be rare.

* (C) Revert #310 and stop accepting values with timezones by default.

  * `2015-01-01 12:00:00-07:00` would be rejected.
  * Pros: Having to explicitly enable time zone support means that there will be no surprises in the field's behavior.
  * Cons: Kinda 1988.

I don't have a definite favorite, but I'm leaning towards B because it represents a kind of best practice. In most cases it's ideal to work in UTC internally; in addition, dropping the redundant `tzinfo` components helps prevent bugs. While this setup would keep the current conflation between `<datetime>` and `<datetime>Z`, it's probably not a big deal, since local datetimes mainly appear in user-facing apps and aware datetimes in APIs.

No particular reason, other than `grad` generally gets more attention than `jacobian`. There's actually more reason to write one for `jacobian`, actually, since you can't do the tuple trick I wrote above (because `jacobian` only works on array arguments).

Using `multigrad` (and the tuple trick with `grad`), both the forward and the backward passes are shared, i.e. there's only one of each. That's unlike the behavior inside `jacobian`, where a separate backward pass is used for each dimension of the output. If you wanted the Jacobian with respect to multiple arguments (i.e. to implement a `multijacobian`), you could also set something up to avoid redundant backward passes, i.e. you would still only require as many backward passes as output dimensions. (You would call a few more VJPs at the end, but you wouldn't be calling `backward_pass` any more.) That might be a good thing to add.

Suppose there is a `grpABCDEF` which has last `SeqId = 10` which has 3 members: `userA`, `userB`, & `userC` where `userA` is topic owner. Suppose all the messages has been delivered to all members devices. 

In other side, the tinode client on user’s device purposely cached the retrieved messages so when user load the topic it could only fetch the latest messages.

Suppose userA hard-delete messages with `SeqId = 5` & `SeqId = 6` while `userB` is online & `userC` is offline. Since it is hard-delete operation, it will be announced to all members of topic. 

Since `userB` is online, he will receive `{pres}` notification so the client could remove the cache. But since `userC` is offline, he won’t receive anything so the deleted messages would still appear on `userC` device while they shouldn't. 

So your suggested solution will not work because client simply doesn’t reload the messages from the beginning every time user load the topic. So I think there should be some kind of notification to offline user about the hard-delete action so the client could remove the cache.

If I remember correctly, Tinode used to have notification feature on `me` topic. So why did this feature removed especially for announcing hard-delete action?

But this commit has been added to others instead of replacing them.

To correct this, you can do:

    git checkout 90f6b13
    git checkout -b new_branch
    git push -f your_remote new_branch:master

where `your_remote` is the remote name of your repository (`origin` if you cloned your fork). Note the `-f` option that's forces the `push` to overwrite previous changes (this is not a good practice on shared repositories, but you're on your fork) ; I think this may be what was missing in your rebase operation.

Hi. I have the same problem too. But I found the real reason.

A [device instance ID](https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/hardware/drivers/install/device-instance-ids) may not have PID, like this.
![snap1](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/1758850/17394777/7b851702-5a5d-11e6-977a-989e782814f4.png)
So this [regex](https://github.com/pyserial/pyserial/blob/master/serial/tools/list_ports_windows.py#L228) will not work and ```info.vid``` ```info.pid``` is ```None```.

If you want to change ```self.vid``` and ```self.pid``` to prevent this problem. You should use ```self.vid if self.vid is not None else 0``` and ```self.pid if self.pid is not None else 0```.
Otherwise you will get a ValueError ```ValueError: Unknown format code 'X' for object of type 'str'```

Here is the specification which states that `PUT` is not specified in json api: http://jsonapi.org/faq/#wheres-put

What the `rest_framework` concerns is that `PUT` is a `PATCH` and they specifically didn't want `PUT` to create resources as well.

See http://www.django-rest-framework.org/api-guide/generic-views/#put-as-create

So if you want to create a resource you need to use `POST`. Closing this issue but if you have questions concerning this please let me know.

Another argument supporting the solution number 1 is this:
Let's say we have 3 models with their relation like this:
`Comment` -> `Post` -> `User`

We have defined serializers for all of them and want the root serializer (the `UserSerializer`) to automatically **include** its related resources. If we only wanted to inlcude the posts, both number 1 and 2 solutions could be seen as fit.

What if we also wanted to **include all** the related **comments** though (second level depth)? In that case I think it's much nicer to use the number 1 approach and define the `UserSerializer` class like this:

```python
class UserSerializer(serializers.Serializer):
    name = serializers.CharField(max_length=200)
    posts = PostSerializer(many=True, read_only=True)

    class JSONAPIMeta:
        resource_name = 'user'
        included_resources = ['posts.comments']
```

Note that all symengine classes deriving from Basic has only one attribute. The pointer to the C++ object.

Cython extension classes like `Basic` has a mechanism similar to `__slots__`. So, if you do `pi.__dict__` it will throw an error. (`pi` is an object of type `Constant` which is a Cython extension class)

All the classes in symengine used to be Cython extension classes and then some were changed to be regular Python classes so that `__new__` could be used for simulating SymPy behaviour of `Add(1, 2)` returning an `Integer` class.

2. Define `__slots__ = ()` in class `Symbol`

Discussing it with @drmarkwslater we thought that perhaps, instead of changing `Executable` too much, it might make sense to add a new application called `Command` which simply runs the supplied command in bash on the WN. So
```python
j = Job()
j.application = Command(run='./myexec.bin')
j.inputfiles = [LocalFile('myexec.bin')]
j.submit()
```
would work. The contents of `Command.run` are executed verbatim. This would generate a shell script to run on the WN of:
```bash
#! /usr/bin/env bash
./myexec.bin
```
`Command` is simpler even than `Executable` as it will never copy over any files.

OK, having a closer look:

- `_pywt.idwt`/`_pywt._idwt` operates on 1D arrays
  - the difference between the two is that `idwt` accepts an `array_like`, and `_idwt` expects a 1D, real valued numpy array.
- `_pywt.idwt_axis` operates along one dimension of a multi-dimensional array
  - There is some code in there to deal with calculating the correct output dimensions and such, so I think a minimal wrapper around the 1D case still has some use.

Personally, I would move `_pywt.idwt` (and `_pywt.dwt`) to pure python for ease of debugging and maintenance, there are no loops or cdefs in it so benefit from Cython is probably minimal. Then there can be 1D versions in `_pywt` that is essentially just a thin wrapper to C plus allocation of the output buffer(s).

you are using the TF Hub library from a command-line tool and would like a
bit more interactive progress tracking, you can set
TFHUB_DOWNLOAD_PROGRESS=1 environment variable to enable progressing
printing into stdout.

Re: the garbage data thing - it's not just the performance impact, it's also the verbosity of the query required when every single access to tag or tensor data has to join Tags + Runs + Experiments + Users tables.  That's every single query, anywhere in TensorBoard, that reads tensor data from the DB.  They would all need to do the joins and a big WHERE clause to ensure the tensor parent entries are still there all the way up to the Users table.

I think that's just the wrong trade-off to be making given that deletion of data will most likely be very rare.  If we can't tolerate any staleness in the data we serve, then I think we should push the extra effort onto the deletion side because those actions are rare and so they can be more verbose (and slower, for whatever performance impact there is), and we should optimize for the query side to be as straightforward and foolproof as possible.

The way this currently works is that all headers that aren't in the 3.0 data model are removed from all MagIC tables prior to upload.  The problem with this approach is that if someone has a slightly incorrect MagIC column, this will be deleted as well. With Rupert's new interface, it's pretty easy to fix a misspelled column name, but a user won't have the option to do that if I chuck that column before the upload.  

Some options:

- We could warn users about the columns that are being auto-deleted.
- We could blacklist instead of whitelisting (drop certain known nuisance columns, but leave ambiguous/potentially misspelled columns in the upload)

Misspelled columns aren't too likely to be a problem for people who are using PmagPy the whole way through their process, since we are pretty consistent.  But if they're mixing and matching with their process, maybe doing some editing by hand, the problem could come up.

3. Other use cases I haven't yet considered.

Since this is (IMO) pretty easily **the biggest known problem with the Pulumi Kubernetes provider,** and since it is definitely necessary to be fixed to complete our product-level objectives, I'd like to propose we move ahead with a fix for this in the next milestone. I'm happy to do it myself.

## The issue

Quick recap:

* It is extremely common for Kubernetes resources to be _created_ but fail to _initialize_ (_e.g._, a `Deployment` object was created but the containers don't have the env vars they need). In most cases they will retry forever.
* In this case, running `pulumi update` will fail, because the resource already exists. Also, it will poll until it times out, which is confusing to the user.

## Proposed solution _for the Kubernetes provider_

I sense that this is a complex issue for AWS, but for Kubernetes, I believe the solution to be relatively simple, because it seems like it should just call `Update` (rather than, say, calling `Delete` and then `Create`).

Distilling an offline conversation with @swgillespie:

* Engine needs to tell service something like “we created this thing, but initialization failed”. This could be implemented as a bool, `initFailure` in the return message of `Create` and `Update`, which is true iff resource creation succeeded and initialization failed.
* The service needs to persist that as part of the checkpoint. Subsequent calls to `pulumi update` should then result in a `Update` rather than a `Create`.
* The core logic of `Update` probably does not need to change, because whether we should delete-and-replace actually depends on which fields were changed, and `Update` already implements this behavior. For example, hard-replacing a `Deployment` will cause all sorts of bad things to happen, one of which is that the `NodePort` is re-allocated; this is not what we want, we should replace it only if specific fields are touched.

This implies a change to the checkpoint schema, and the gRPC interface, so I want to make sure we're on the same page that this is a good way to go.

Given the importance of landing this fix, I'm hoping to get sign off relatively soon. cc @swgillespie, @lukehoban, @pgavlin

```
	"num_workers": 12,
	"sample_batch_size": 2000,
	"timesteps_per_batch": 5000,
```

So here, each worker will return a sample batch of 2000 steps. The driver will only wait for 5000 steps before continuing with optimization. This means that only 6000 of the 24000 steps per iteration are actually used for optimization!

In 0.5.0, we counted all the steps. In 0.5.2, we only count the steps used by the optimizer.

Some notes:

I think the way to do this is to provide a hook for the socket and socketpair constructors, and for getaddrinfo and getnameinfo, and put the bulk of the code in a separate library. (The other constructors like from_stdlib_socket would remain unhooked. This is probably important for subprocess support, as well as being the Right Thing I think.)

The getaddrinfo hook should probably only be called after we check the numeric fast path and go through idna encoding, so it would always receive the hostname as a bytestring. (It can also receive the hostname as a numeric IP, iff the service is a symbolic name.)

A useful fake network probably needs to include features like:

* Special casing `localhost` and the numeric equivalents.

* Built in behaviors like IPs that work, that fail fast, that act as black holes. (There may be further subdivisions like, accept connections but then acts as a black hole, or accept connections but then error out when sending data? I guess error out on send is easily doable by putting up a fake server so maybe that's not needed at the network level.)

* Ability to make assertions about what operations happened, and when

* A sensible default set of mappings from hostnames to IPs, and from IPs/ports to behaviors.

* The ability to manipulate these mappings to set up canned configurations. (Possibly this should be a bit higher level than just "here's the desired output from `getaddrinfo`? `getaddrinfo` is pretty complicated when you get into all the different flags and protocol types.)

I'm not sure to what kind of behavior plugin API we want. Something like the memory_stream objects, with callbacks that fire to pump data? An active "switchboard" that gets notified of connection attempts etc and then decides what to do? Just a small set of canned behaviors + for actual interaction you can put up a fake server to talk to with whatever complex behavior you want? (This could even use an extended interface, e.g imagine a version `accept` that let you peek at the connection attempt and then decide what to do with it.)

I would say the pitch of #16 is "you can use `await yield_from_` in a native async generator with native `yield` statements", like the async_generator package previously supported on Linux but not Windows. Being able to use `await yield_` too is an unintended and mostly-harmless side effect.

(And if you leave off the `@async_generator` from an async function that uses _only_ `await yield_` and/or `await yield_from_`, and doesn't use any native `yield` statements, you get not an async generator but a normal async function, that will hopelessly confuse your event loop unless you happen to call it from another async generator -- i.e., you have written something that fits in a similar place as `yield_()` itself. Everything in this parenthetical is the same with or without #16, since there are no async generators involved.)
----
And, of course, the other half of the pitch for #16 is that it's required in order for #17 to work. I think I agree with you that we shouldn't commit #16 if we don't intend to commit some form of #17. I think that to the extent there's a case to be made for committing both of them, it goes like so:

If you're writing an async application that targets Python 3.6+ only, you're probably going to use native async generators, because they take less typing to write / are faster to execute / don't add lots of traceback frames / are arguably syntactically clearer. If your application uses an async library that wants to support 3.5, that library is probably going to use `@async_generator` functions, because it kinda has to. Now within your Python interpreter are two different beasts called "async generator", which behave in mostly but not entirely identical ways. That seems to me to be a recipe for confusion. My overall goal with this set of PRs (#15, #16, #17) has been to reduce the confusion by making `@async_generator` functions behave as much like native async generators as the constraints of the underlying implementation will permit. Making them identical under the hood seems to be about as much alike as we could possibly achieve. I think this is a good idea, though obviously it's allowed to compete with other good ideas like "don't depend on internal implementation details".
----
Regarding PyPy support: Apparently PyPy has reasonably solid 3.6 support on nightly these days, including async generators. I played around with its async generators and, via a similar type-punning scheme to the one used in this PR for CPython, got my hands on something that appears to be an AsyncGenValueWrapper object. Unfortunately, any attempt to use it beyond id() segfaults. My understanding of the PyPy object memory layout is based on a blog post from 2009, so it's not unreasonable that these difficulties might be resolved with a less cursory effort. (Very much not guaranteed to work out, though.)

I think #17 can be modified to make it transparent without much trouble. Currently it is very loud and conservative about the one tiny difference it's aware of (the ag_running thing), but I think it would be reasonable for us to just declare that if "ag_running is False while an asend is active but suspended" is good enough for CPython, it's good enough for us. As dicey as bytecode introspection can be in the general case, this one is looking for an extremely simple pattern with no potential for false positives -- maybe some wacky stack management could make us fail to detect an asyncgen that is truly safe, but we'll never think something is safe (returns only None) when it has non-None return paths. And the mechanics of the code object reconstruction is exactly the same thing that the stdlib `inspect` module does to set CO_ITERABLE_COROUTINE. Not that that inspires _complete_ confidence, but at least it's not nothing. :-)

Any thoughts? I'm inclined to push a little more on seeing whether I can make this native integration strategy work with PyPy, and will feel less bullish about these diffs if I can't.

I am okay with removing LpNormalization but there is/can be a difference between ReduceL2 and L2Normalization. 

ReduceL2 is ``sqrt{sum(x_i**2)}`` where ``0<= i <=n``

and L2Normalization is  ``x_i / sqrt{sum(x_i**2)}`` where ``0<= i <=n``

The above definition is for 1-d arrays, but similar logic can be extended to high dimension arrays with different axes.
